Statistical Analysis of Online Shopping Behavior and Purchase Conversion

MAS291 Computer Project

Lương Triều Vỹ – QE200035
Phạm Bửu Thịnh – QE200023
Nguyễn Đức Duy – QE200054
Nguyễn Quốc Vương – QE200066
Đinh Đỗ Gia Huy – QE200022

20 Tháng Bảy 2026

1 Introduction

This project analyzes online shopping sessions from the UCI Online Shoppers Purchasing Intention Dataset. The population of interest is online shopping activity on an e-commerce platform. The observed dataset contains 12,330 independent sessions. Revenue divides the data into 1,908 purchase sessions and 10,422 non-purchase sessions.

The analysis studies engagement duration, conversion, differences between purchase outcomes, and the relationship between product-page count and total product-page duration.

1.1 Full dataset evidence - 12,330 sessions

The table below contains the complete dataset used in the project. Session_ID is the sequential identifier created for each online shopping session. The final three binary columns (Revenue_int, Weekend_int, and Returning_int) were derived for statistical calculations. Use the search box, column filters and page controls to inspect all 12,330 observations without expanding the report vertically.

2 Task 1 - Exploratory Data Analysis

2.1 Structure and data quality

Measure Result Interpretation
Rows 12330 One row per online session
Original variables 18 Original UCI variables, including Revenue
Missing cells 0 No missing values expected
Duplicate rows 125 Exact duplicate feature vectors; retained for analysis
Purchase sessions 1908 Revenue = TRUE
Non-purchase sessions 10422 Revenue = FALSE
Minimum group size 1908 Exceeds required 500 observations

There are no missing values. Exact duplicate feature vectors are reported but retained because sessions with identical behavior may still represent genuine observations. Both groups exceed the required minimum of 500 observations.

Purchase sessions view more product pages and spend more time on product-related pages on average. Non-purchase sessions have higher bounce and exit rates. Means exceed medians for several behavioral variables, while positive skewness and high-end IQR outliers show strong right-skewness.

2.3 Visualizations

In the interval chart, each color is normalized within its own purchase-outcome group, so the blue bars together sum to 100% and the red bars together sum to 100%. The duration distribution is concentrated at lower values with a long upper tail. Purchase sessions have a larger share in longer-duration intervals, but the extreme observations make the mean less representative than the median alone. The boxplot display is limited to 6,000 seconds for readability; more extreme observations remain included in all calculations and their IQR counts are reported in the descriptive-statistics table.

Each monthly bar is calculated independently as purchase sessions divided by all sessions observed in that month, so the bars are not components that should sum to 100%. Purchase outcome also varies across visitor types. These descriptive differences are associations and should not be interpreted as causal effects.

3 Task 2 - Inference for One Population

3.1 Population mean

We test whether mean product-page duration differs from 1,200 seconds using a two-sided one-sample t procedure at the 5% significance level.

Measure Value
n 12330
Sample mean 1194.74621996883
Sample SD 1913.669287872
Hypothesized mean 1200
t statistic -0.304850236850555
Degrees of freedom 12329
Two-sided p-value 0.760485363690732
95% CI lower 1160.96494182988
95% CI upper 1228.52749810778
Decision Fail to reject H0

The estimated mean is approximately 1,194.7 seconds. The p-value is greater than 0.05, so there is insufficient evidence that the population mean differs from 1,200 seconds.

3.2 Population proportion

We test whether the conversion proportion differs from 15% using a large-sample two-sided z procedure.

Measure Value
Sessions n 12330
Purchases x 1908
Sample proportion 0.154744525547445
Hypothesized proportion 0.15
z statistic 1.47543212553581
Two-sided p-value 0.140096403745452
95% CI lower 0.148360888364276
95% CI upper 0.161128162730615
Decision Fail to reject H0

The estimated conversion rate is approximately 15.47%. The p-value is greater than 0.05, so there is insufficient evidence that conversion differs from the selected 15% benchmark.

4 Task 3 - Comparison of Two Populations

4.1 Difference between two means

Purchase and non-purchase sessions are compared using Welch’s independent-samples t procedure because group sizes and variances differ.

Measure Value
Purchase n 1908
Purchase mean 1876.2096148889
Purchase SD 2312.21439167297
No-purchase n 10422
No-purchase mean 1069.98780915444
No-purchase SD 1803.79775675192
Difference 806.221805734462
Welch t 14.4469889975526
Welch df 2350.27345415916
Two-sided p-value 0.00000000000000000000000000000000000000000000217379104377348
95% CI lower 696.788630216247
95% CI upper 915.654981252676
Decision Reject H0

Purchase sessions spend approximately 806 additional seconds on product-related pages. The difference is statistically significant and practically substantial.

4.2 Difference between two proportions

The returning-visitor proportions of purchase and non-purchase sessions are compared using a pooled z test and an unpooled confidence interval.

Measure Value
Purchase returning count 1470
Purchase n 1908
Purchase proportion 0.770440251572327
No-purchase returning count 9081
No-purchase n 10422
No-purchase proportion 0.8713298791019
Difference -0.100889627529573
Pooled proportion 0.855717761557178
z statistic -11.5307615438008
Two-sided p-value 0.000000000000000000000000000000923234010878834
95% CI lower -0.120824730711875
95% CI upper -0.0809545243472704
Decision Reject H0

The returning-visitor shares differ significantly. This comparison concerns group composition and does not by itself prove that new visitors have a higher individual probability of purchase.

5 Task 4 - Simple Linear Regression

X is the number of product-related pages viewed, and Y is total product-page duration in seconds. These variables form an interpretable engagement model: viewing more product pages should generally require more total browsing time.

The scatter plot shows a strong positive and approximately linear relationship, with increasing spread at high page counts.

Measure Value
Observations n 12330
Correlation r 0.86092683617637
R squared 0.741195017248654
Intercept 19.3009166234263
Slope 37.0435211086784
Residual standard error 973.578147173377
Slope standard error 0.197145114622822
Slope t statistic 187.899767029734
Slope p-value 0
Slope 95% lower 36.6570858440866
Slope 95% upper 37.4299563732703
Decision Reject H0

The fitted equation is approximately Y-hat = 19.30 + 37.04X. Each additional product page is associated with about 37 additional seconds of product-page time on average. The slope is statistically different from zero. The model measures engagement mechanics and should not be interpreted as a causal model of purchase completion.

5.1 Predictions

ProductRelated fit lwr upr
10 389.74 370.61 408.86
25 945.39 928.01 962.77
50 1871.48 1852.90 1890.06
100 3723.65 3692.17 3755.14
200 7428.01 7360.75 7495.26

6 Task 5 - Final Interpretation

6.1 Key findings

Analysis Finding
EDA 1908 purchase and 10422 non-purchase sessions; no missing values; right-skewed behavioral measures with high-end outliers.
One mean Mean duration 1194.7 seconds; 95% CI 1161.0 to 1228.5; p = 0.7605 against 1,200 seconds.
One proportion Conversion 15.47%; 95% CI 14.84% to 16.11%; p = 0.1401 against 15%.
Two means Buyers spend 806.2 seconds longer on product pages; 95% CI 696.8 to 915.7; p < .001.
Two proportions Returning-visitor share difference -10.1 percentage points; 95% CI -12.1 to -8.1; p < .001.
Regression r = 0.861, R-squared = 74.1%, Y-hat = 19.30 + 37.04X; slope p < .001.

The duration difference is both statistically significant and practically large: converted sessions spend roughly 13.4 additional minutes on product pages. The visitor-composition difference is statistically significant but requires cautious interpretation. The regression relationship is practically strong because product-page count explains a large proportion of engagement-time variation. In contrast, the overall mean and conversion rate are close to their selected benchmarks and are not statistically significant.

6.2 Conclusions and implications

Number Conclusion
1 Deeper product exploration is strongly associated with successful conversion.
2 High bounce and exit behavior is concentrated more strongly among non-purchase sessions.
3 Product-page count predicts engagement time but does not establish purchase causation.
4 Visitor-type conversion rates should be examined before targeting returning versus new visitors.
5 A/B tests are needed to establish causality; this observational dataset supports association and inference.

6.3 Limitations

The dataset covers anonymized sessions and does not include transaction amount, demographic information, or a repeat-user identifier. Several variables are strongly skewed, and the two purchase groups are unbalanced. The analyses rely on independence and large-sample assumptions. Observational associations do not establish causality; controlled experiments such as A/B tests are required for causal platform decisions.

7 Data Source

UCI Machine Learning Repository, Online Shoppers Purchasing Intention Dataset:
https://archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/468/online+shoppers+purchasing+intention+dataset
